_status: SIGNED-OFF 2026-07-13 — owner-authorized go-live (3rd batch). Class I-II (II+ to III at high water)
name: Mulberry River
region: Ozark National Forest / Boston Mountains, NW Arkansas (Franklin, Johnson & Crawford Counties)
difficultyRating: Class I-II (II+ to III at high water)
description: The Mulberry is Arkansas's premier float and whitewater river: a ~70-mile, free-flowing (undammed), spring- and rain-fed stream that drops out of the Boston Mountains of the Ozark National Forest and runs generally west-southwest to the Arkansas River near the town of Mulberry. It offers scenic Ozark canyon floating with tree-lined bluffs and reliable Class I-II rapids that stiffen to II+/III at higher water. It is notoriously FLASHY - rising and falling several feet within a day after rain - and is typically runnable only from late October to mid-June, often becoming too low to float in the hot summer months. Popular access runs cluster on the upper/middle river along Highway 215 (Wolf Pen, Byrd's, High Bank, Redding) down to Turner Bend at Cass on Highway 23.
managingAuthority: U.S. Forest Service - Ozark-St. Francis National Forest (Boston Mountain & Pleasant Hill Ranger Districts). Also a component of the National Wild & Scenic Rivers System (designated 1992) and an Arkansas Natural & Scenic River / Extraordinary Resource Water (state-designated 1985).
accessLaw: Arkansas navigable-waters access: the Mulberry is a legally navigable stream, so the public may float, fish, and wade on the water and below the ordinary high-water mark. However, the streambed banks and adjacent land are largely private or National Forest - there is no general right to trespass on private banks. Put in / take out only at established public accesses (USFS recreation areas such as Wolf Pen, Redding, and outfitter landings such as Byrd's and Turner Bend); some private accesses charge a fee.

research.calibrationKeySource: Turner Bend staff-gauge key transferred to USGS datum (optimal, 3 cross-checks) + American Whitewater USGS-gauge levels + NWS AHPS flood stages (danger)
research.calibrationNotes: UNIT REASONING: USGS 07252000 reports both cfs (~283 today) and gage height (1.66 ft today). I chose FEET because every authoritative floatability source (Turner Bend, Byrd's, American Whitewater) and the NWS flood ladder is expressed in feet; the community publishes essentially no cfs floatability thresholds. CRITICAL TWO-GAUGE CAVEAT: the outfitter floatability key (too_low..optimal_max) originates on Turner Bend's OWN staff gauge at their Cass landing, a DIFFERENT physical gauge ~23 river-miles UPSTREAM of USGS 07252000, with a different stage datum (Turner Bend's channel 'floods'/refunds above ~4.5-5 ft; USGS 07252000 doesn't reach flood until 18 ft). The transfer of Turner Bend's ft numbers onto the USGS datum is justified by THREE consistent cross-checks in the ~1.5-3.6 ft range (prime 3.0-3.5 ft AW/USGS = 3.1-3.6 ft Turner Bend; USGS 1.66 ft = summer-low; USGS 2.54 ft = 'good'), but is NOT validated above ~3.6 ft, where the two gauges diverge. HIGH-CONFIDENCE anchors native to USGS 07252000 (NWS AHPS / MLBA4, NAVD88 datum ~433 ft): action stage 10 ft, minor flood 18 ft, moderate flood 20 ft, major flood 22 ft. OBSERVED stage-flow pairs (for rough cfs cross-reference only): 1.67 ft=283 cfs, 1.78 ft=348 cfs, 2.54 ft=830 cfs; all-time peak 41,300 cfs; median ~38 cfs. CLASSIC RULE OF THUMB: floatable roughly 1.5-4 ft (prime 2.5-3.5 ft); below ~1.2 ft you drag (kayaks only); >~4 ft is pushy, >8 ft experts-only; NWS flood at 18 ft. GAPS: high (6 ft) is LOW confidence; a true cfs-keyed floatability ladder could not be sourced. If the app can add a manual/secondary reference, Turner Bend's landing gauge is the single most authoritative floatability source but has no public API/site ID. It is currently summer-low and the river is 'generally not floatable in the hot summer months.'
hazards: Flash flooding: very flashy, undammed river; can rise several feet within hours after rain (all-time USGS peak 41,300 cfs vs ~283 cfs today). Never launch on a rising gauge or with heavy rain forecast upstream.

Low-water bridge(s) near Turner Bend/Redding: 'stay to the right to get under the Low Water Bridge'; becomes an impassable, dangerous strainer at high water (Turner Bend refuses rentals once too high to get under it, ~4.5 ft on their gauge).

Named rapids that stiffen and can swamp/pin boats at higher flows: Whoop and Holler (big-wave playspot at 6-8+ ft on USGS gauge), Hell Roaring Falls, Hamm Falls (Hamm's Fall), Big Al's Twist, Chainsaw Jungle, Jump Start, Troll Shoal, Sacroiliac Rapid.
Strainers / fallen trees, notably in the second mile below Turner Bend; tight curves at high water that can pin or damage boats.
Class jumps to II+/III at high water; upper Wolf Pen-to-Redding reach is Class II+ and Redding-to-Turner Bend can be Class III at high flows - not for beginners when pushy.
Summer low water: below ~1.2 ft (gauge) most riffles must be dragged or portaged; river is often not floatable June-October.
Remote Ozark canyon with limited cell service; cold water hazard early in the season (spring/late fall).
